flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of adding a vertex to an adjacency matrix representation?
flashcards.back: O(V²) because the entire matrix may need to be reallocated or resized to accommodate the new vertex.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of adding a vertex to an adjacency list representation?
flashcards.back: O(1) if we simply add a new entry to the list. However, resizing may take O(V+E) time.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between BFS and DFS traversal?
flashcards.back: BFS uses a queue and explores level by level (breadth-first), while DFS uses a stack (or recursion) and explores depth-first.

flashcards.front: How do you check if two vertices are adjacent in an adjacency matrix?
flashcards.back: Check if matrix[u][v] is 1 (or non-zero for weighted graphs). This operation takes O(1) time.

flashcards.front: How do you check if two vertices are adjacent in an adjacency list?
flashcards.back: Traverse the linked list at index u and search for vertex v. This takes O(degree(u)) time in worst case.

flashcards.front: What is the space complexity of adjacency matrix for a graph with V vertices?
flashcards.back: O(V²) - requires a V×V matrix regardless of the number of edges.

flashcards.front: What is the space complexity of adjacency list for a graph with V vertices and E edges?
flashcards.back: O(V + E) - stores V lists plus E edge entries.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of DFS traversal?
flashcards.back: O(V + E) - each vertex is visited once and each edge is examined once.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of BFS traversal?
flashcards.back: O(V + E) - each vertex is enqueued once and each edge is examined once.

flashcards.front: What is a spanning tree of a graph?
flashcards.back: A spanning tree is a subgraph that is a tree and includes all vertices of the original graph with minimum possible edges (V-1 edges).

flashcards.front: What is the difference between a directed and undirected graph operation?
flashcards.back: In undirected graphs, edge (u,v) appears in both adjacency[u] and adjacency[v]. In directed graphs, it appears only in adjacency[u] (from u to v).
Graph union creates a new graph containing all vertices and edges from both graphs. If both graphs have an edge between same vertices, the edge appears once in the result.
Graph intersection creates a new graph containing only vertices and edges that exist in both original graphs.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of marking vertices as visited during traversal?
flashcards.back: Marking prevents infinite loops when encountering cycles and ensures each vertex is processed exactly once.

flashcards.front: What is a connected component in an undirected graph?
flashcards.back: A connected component is a maximal set of vertices where each pair is connected by a path, and no vertex in the set is connected to any vertex outside the set.
